title: Jamaican curry chicken
subtitle: Quick Jamaican-style chicken curry with coconut milk and potato
excerpt: Bone-in chicken pieces simmered in Jamaican curry powder with coconut milk, potato, and a whole scotch bonnet. Lighter and quicker than curry goat, with the same characteristic spice profile.
sourceNotes: Jamaican curry chicken is a home-kitchen staple derived from the curry tradition brought by East Indian indentured workers in the nineteenth century. It is faster than curry goat and uses the same Jamaican curry powder profile, with coconut milk added for creaminess in the home cook version. The potato is an optional but common addition that absorbs the gravy and acts as a carbohydrate within the dish itself.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to 3 months. The potato may soften further on reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The finished curry keeps in the fridge for 3 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Marinate the chicken up to 24 hours ahead. The finished curry improves after a day.

content.text: Jamaican curry chicken is weeknight cooking: it comes together in under an hour and the whole pot can be on the table inside fifty minutes of starting. The key difference from South Asian chicken curry is the curry powder — Jamaican blends are typically milder, more fragrant, and include allspice, which adds the characteristic pimento warmth. The potato soaks up the gravy from the inside out, becoming dense and intensely flavoured by the time the chicken is done.

Use bone-in thighs and drumsticks for the best result; boneless chicken dries out before the gravy has a chance to develop. Coat the chicken in curry powder and salt before you heat the pan — the spice will bloom in the fat rather than going directly into the liquid.

Toss the chicken pieces with {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide heavy pot over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken in batches, skin-side down first, for 4 minutes per side until golden. Remove to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, and {{spring-onion}} spring onions; cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme sprigs.

Return the chicken to the pot. Pour over the {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and 200 ml of water. Add the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over medium-low heat for 20 minutes. Add the potato chunks, stir gently, and continue cooking for another 20–25 minutes until the potato is tender and the chicken is cooked through. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme before serving.

items.cause: Lid left off; too much water added
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 10 minutes and increase the heat to medium. Stir occasionally until the gravy thickens

items.cause: Added too late or heat too low
items.fix: Check the potato is cut into even 4 cm pieces. Increase to a medium simmer and add 5–10 minutes to the cook time

items.symptom: Curry paste sitting on the surface
items.cause: Spice not cooked into the fat before liquid was added
items.fix: When making again, bloom the curry powder in the hot oil for 30 seconds before adding the aromatics. For this batch, stir vigorously to distribute

items.cause: Only one tablespoon of curry powder used
items.fix: Jamaican curry chicken typically needs 2 heaped tablespoons per kilo of chicken. Add a teaspoon more and cook for 5 extra minutes to integrate it

content.text: Jamaican curry chicken sits in the weeknight rotation alongside brown stew chicken. It is lighter and faster than curry goat but shares the same essential spice character. The coconut milk version is most common in the diaspora; in Jamaica, many cooks make a drier version without coconut milk, relying on the braising liquid from the chicken alone. Either way, it is served with white rice or rice and peas and a piece of roti or hard dough bread to mop up the gravy.
